mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover Classic V8 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Classic V8 engine typically costs £1,200–£4,200 supply only depending on displacement (3.5, 3.9, or 4.2). Fitting adds £800–£1,700. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Range Rover Classic V8 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which variant you have. The 3.5-litre (carb or EFI) is widely regarded as the most robust – simple, under-stressed, and well-understood. The 3.9 and 4.2 offer more power but bring liner and head gasket vulnerabilities, particularly if the cooling system has been neglected.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Range Rover Classic V8?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common issues are cooling system neglect (hoses, radiators, water pumps), cylinder head gasket failure on 3.9 and 4.2 engines, and liner slippage on the 4.2 in particular. Early 3.5 carburettor engines can suffer from carburettor wear and ignition system issues.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover Classic V8 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a tidy £10,000–£20,000 Classic Range Rover, a £2,500 recon is 12–25% of the car's value – well worth it. Against a rusty £5,000 example, the same recon may exceed our 60% Land Rover threshold – get a chassis and cooling system inspection before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Range Rover Classic's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine size (3.5, 3.9, or 4.2) is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C. As a rule of thumb: 1970–1989 is 3.5 (carb until 1984, EFI from 1984), 1990–1992 is 3.9 EFI, and 1992–1995 LWB/LSE models are 4.2 EFI.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair a Classic V8 engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – cooling system neglect, head gasket failure, ignition system wear – are repairs, not full replacements. The Rover V8 is a relatively straightforward engine to work on, and parts are available through specialists. Major internal failure (liner slippage, crank failure) would typically justify replacement over rebuild.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect Classic Range Rover V8 pricing. The 4.2 typically costs more than the 3.5/3.9 due to rarity and LWB/LSE application. Older generations: see the Era Map above for generation-specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Classic Range Rover V8 engine typically takes 12–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent Land Rover specialist).

problems.title: Cooling System Neglect / Overheating
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, poor heater performance, pressurisation of the cooling system, steam from under the bonnet.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — age-related (hoses, radiators, water pumps are 25–30+ years old).
problems.repairCost: £300–£1,200 [PLACEHOLDER] (cooling system overhaul, radiator, water pump, hoses).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you see overheating or steam, stop driving immediately.

problems.recommendation: The Rover V8 is fundamentally robust, but the cooling system is now decades old. Neglected cooling systems are the single biggest killer of these engines — thermostats failing closed, hoses perishing, radiators clogging. Regular coolant changes and visual inspection of hoses are essential. If you see any sign of overheating, investigate immediately — a £300–£1,200 cooling system overhaul is far cheaper than a £2,200+ engine replacement.

problems.title: Cylinder Head Gasket Failure (3.9 and 4.2)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, oil in coolant (mayonnaise), white smoke from exhaust, overheating, loss of power.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if head gasket failure is suspected.

problems.recommendation: The 3.9 and 4.2 engines are more prone to head gasket failure than the 3.5, particularly if the cooling system has been neglected. The aluminium block and iron liners expand at different rates, and overheating exacerbates this. On the 4.2, the dry liners can become "wafer thin" when bored out, increasing the risk. A compression test and coolant system pressure test will confirm. If caught early, head gasket replacement is a manageable specialist job.

problems.title: Liner Slippage / Block Damage (3.9 and 4.2)
problems.symptoms: Coolant in oil, persistent overheating, oil pressure loss, knocking from the engine.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: stop driving immediately if you hear knocking or see coolant in oil.

problems.recommendation: On the 3.9 and particularly the 4.2, the pressed-in wet liners can slip or drop, causing catastrophic failure. This is most common on engines that have been overheated or subjected to high mileage. If you're buying a 3.9 or 4.2, factor in the possibility of liner issues. A block that has dropped a liner is usually scrap — replacement is the only sensible option.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover Classic V8 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Cost-effective on any Classic worth keeping
Replacement often more cost-effective than block repair
Worth doing during a rebuild or as preventative maintenance
Full engine replacement often more cost-effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Range Rover Classic V8? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented cooling system history — the single most important factor
* A well-maintained 3.5 (carb or EFI) — the most robust of the three
* Evidence of regular oil changes (every 3,000–5,000 miles)
* A 3.9 or 4.2 with recent head gasket and cooling system work
avoidIf: * Any sign of overheating or coolant loss — these are expensive to diagnose
* A 4.2 with no documented engine work — liner issues are a real risk
* Poor service history — these engines are intolerant of neglect
* Any evidence of liner slippage (coolant in oil, persistent overheating)

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Classic Range Rover V8 is one of the great British engines — an aluminium-block OHV V8 that's fundamentally robust but demands respect for its age. The 3.5 is the most reliable of the three; the 3.9 and 4.2 offer more power but bring liner and head gasket vulnerabilities. Buy on cooling system history, not on mileage. A well-maintained Classic V8 is a rewarding ownership proposition; a neglected one will punish you with bills that may exceed the vehicle's value.

cta.label: → Compare Range Rover Classic V8 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover Classic V8 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Any smoke? Any unusual noise from the top end?
2. Service History — Cooling system work is the single most important thing to check
3. Coolant Condition — Any oil in coolant? Any sign of pressurisation?
4. Oil Condition — Any coolant in oil (mayonnaise)? Any metallic particles?
5. Cooling System Age — When were the hoses, water pump, and radiator last replaced?
6. Overheating History — Has the vehicle ever overheated? (Critical on 3.9/4.2)
7. Engine Warning Light — On EFI models, have codes been read?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures or structural rot (chassis, bulkhead)

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly.
